## 04.04.2025

A brief update on TLS-IDS tcycif, now we have images of 9 cycles for all
slides except for a dried one with 7 cycles. I finished antibody
accessment and in general the staining and image quality are quite good.
AICDA_1, CD45_1, IgD_1 and PAX8_10 have lower quality with different
issues - messy background, weak signal and probably unspecific binding,
but we will figure it out during later quality control and gating. I

8_10 have lower quality with different
issues - messy background, weak signal and probably unspecific binding,
but we will figure it out during later quality control and gating. I
think these issues of these antibodies are within our expectation
without any surprise. I don\'t believe we should redo any staining or
add more cycles. I discussed with Ada and now we will continue with
stitching and segmentation. I already organized folder structures for
the raw data in P drive
P:\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Data\\7_TLS_tCycIF\\Exp_4\\1_raw_data

## 07.04.2025

Segmentation test from Ada

[MCMICRO is experiencing quite many difficulties to be executed. Thanks
to ziqi I was able to open a QC file from the segmentation, which is not
the actual mask but the prediction done from the model. I think it is
enough information to tell that it overperforms stardist for the TLSs
but the result still is far from good, with many areas undersegmented,
especially in the GC]{.mark}

[Regarding the local storage, I was discussing with Ziqi and she was
pretty sure that because of the university firewall system we cannot run
anything in the drives. Actually that is what we do now for the basic,
which runs in docker. I\'ll still report my errors and message Yu-An
tomorrow. I have some small crops we can send him as well. My concern is
that even in the best-case scenario with MCMICRO, we'd need 1--2 weeks

report my errors and message Yu-An
tomorrow. I have some small crops we can send him as well. My concern is
that even in the best-case scenario with MCMICRO, we'd need 1--2 weeks
for optimization and another 2 weeks just to transfer the data locally.
All this without including the actual segmentation time.]{.mark}

Mesmer is the only tool we can confidently rely on this scenario. I
think we should give it a try to ask someone else in the lab to help
optimize it on WSI. Lina already has some scripts, and with the right
person, it could be done in about a week.

## 15.04.2025

TLS project meeting

Today\'s meeting notes:

1\. TLS Segmentation Strategy

- Mesmer vs. Stardist:

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 > compared to Stardist.

 > Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 > needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 > Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells
 > in overlapping regions.

 > Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error -- better
 > skipping and need to filter or avoid black tiles.

 > Benefits of Mesmer:

 - Performs more accurately on thicker tissue sections.

 > This improved performance will benefit future projects,
 > including proteopicks using thicker sections.

 > Optimization Plan:

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the

formance will benefit future projects,
 > including proteopicks using thicker sections.

 > Optimization Plan:

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the
 > Mesmer-based segmentation pipeline. Lina has scripts that
 > could support this process.

 > Backup Plan:

 - If whole-slide segmentation is not feasible, fallback will be to
 > crop individual TLS regions and segment them using Mesmer
 > separately.

2\. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations

- 4 μm sections:

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 > segmentation of mature TLS structures.

 > 5 μm sections (current study):

 - Chosen to collect more material for GeoMx analysis, causing
 > segmentation issues in dense areas like TLSs.

 > General Note:

 - Thinner sections are preferable for H&E staining.

 > Thicker sections are more suitable for RNA/protein profiling.

 > A future reporting system is applied to track section
 > thickness (in μm) used per experiment.

3\. Action Points & Next Steps

- By next Friday, Ziqi will assess feasibility of Mesmer WSI
 > optimization and decide whether the current plan needs to be
 > adjusted. Ada will assist Ziqi with all necessary existing
 > materials on the meeting of next Tuesday 22th. Zhihan will also
 > follow and assist with segmentation optimization.

 > Hanna will contact:

t Ziqi with all necessary existing
 > materials on the meeting of next Tuesday 22th. Zhihan will also
 > follow and assist with segmentation optimization.

 > Hanna will contact:

 - Oskari for H&E-based scripts to identify lymphoid aggregates and
 > TLSs, and try to generate the quantitative data.

 > Joona for biological thresholds and TLSs / Immune aggs
 > definitions to help with classification.

 > Zhihan will ask Anastasia to check for normal/benign cases in the
 > Oncosys-Ova cohort to obtain normal omentum samples as negative
 > controls. We may get around 3-5 samples within our cohort.

## 22.04.2025

Ziqi and Ada's meeting for segmentation test

We assessed together the output that Lina obtained with her last script.
Already we can tell it is viable to tile and untile. The main issues
were (1) time of run - it took 2 days to segment this image, (2)
malfunctioning while passing totally black tiles. You can see that the
image looks great but the edges are full of masked individual pixels.Our
hypothesis is that these two issues are interconnected - full black
tiles are hard to handle and consume a lot of time to be masked.

Ziqi will work on a way to avoid passing these to MESMER, and she will
implement some benchmarks (time per tile, time per image etc). I will
assist with big errors if any. We will re-evaluate based on her results

passing these to MESMER, and she will
implement some benchmarks (time per tile, time per image etc). I will
assist with big errors if any. We will re-evaluate based on her results
the strategies and things to try next week.

## 28.04.2025

Ziqi and Ada's meeting for segmentation test

The segmentation progress is looking quite optimistic. With Ziqi\'s new
system we will save around 1/3 of the time. She sees this as a viable
option, we need to prepare for slow computation though but there are
still some ways to make it better. (1) further optimizing the thresholds
to avoid segmenting background, (2) parallelizing.

## 30.04.2025

## Segmentation Test - MESMER

Codes are in Github

Test files are here:
P:\\h30492\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Segmentation_test\\STARDIST_AND_MESMER

Based on Ziqi, Zhihan and Ada's work:

### **1. Image Preprocessing Workflow**

- Multichannel TIFF images were processed using:

 - **Gamma correction** (gamma=γ) to enhance contrast.

 - **Unsharp masking** (sigma=σ, strength=s) to sharpen nuclear
 > features.

- Channels (e.g., DAPI, membrane) were stacked into a 3D array format
 > and reshaped to comply with MESMER's input shape: (1, H, W, C).

### **2. Tiling Strategy**

- Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size
 > and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage.

ape: (1, H, W, C).

### **2. Tiling Strategy**

- Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size
 > and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage.

- This produced a tile array of shape (B, num_x, num_y, tile_x,
 > tile_y, C).

### **3. Filtering Background Tiles with GMM and Other Metrics**

To optimize nuclear segmentation by avoiding background-only regions,
several strategies were tested for identifying and excluding
low-information tiles before running MESMER. These methods aimed to
reduce unnecessary computation and improve segmentation quality.

#### **3.1 Baseline Thresholding with Mean Intensity**

- **Method**: Calculated the **mean pixel intensity** of each tile on
 > the DAPI channel (channel_idx=0).

- **Threshold**: Tiles with mean \< 3500 were excluded from
 > segmentation.

- **Outcome**: Performed well on images with consistent staining but
 > failed in cases with uneven illumination or high signal
 > heterogeneity.

#### **3.2 Comparison with Sum-Based Filtering**

- **Method**: Used np.sum() of DAPI intensity values instead of the
 > mean.

- **Observation**: Performed poorly despite identical tile sizes.
 > Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more
 > sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to
 > misclassification of low-content tiles.

s.
 > Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more
 > sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to
 > misclassification of low-content tiles.

- **Conclusion**: np.mean was more robust due to normalization by
 > pixel count.

#### **3.3 Feature Combination: Mean + Standard Deviation**

- **Method**: Combined **mean** and **standard deviation** (std) of
 > DAPI intensities per tile into 2D feature vectors.

- **Goal**: Capture both brightness and texture/variance of tiles.

- **Input to GMM**: X = \[\[mean₁, std₁\], \[mean₂, std₂\], \...,
 > \[meanₙ, stdₙ\]\]

- **Result**: Improved separation of background vs. foreground in
 > heterogeneous images.

#### **3.4 Median Intensity Filtering**

- **Trial**: Replaced mean with **median intensity** as a more
 > outlier-robust statistic.

- **Result**: Median has a close result compared to mthe ean.

#### **3.5 Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) Clustering**

- **Method**: Applied sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture to intensity
 > features (mean or \[mean + std\]) across all tiles.

- **Parameters**:

 - n_components = 3 (assumed: background, low-signal, high-signal)

 - random_state = 0 for reproducibility

- **Label Assignment**:

 - Automatically determined the cluster with the **lowest mean** as
 > background (via
 > filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background)

ducibility

- **Label Assignment**:

 - Automatically determined the cluster with the **lowest mean** as
 > background (via
 > filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background)

 - Background labels were excluded from downstream prediction
